providerReplies.body: Thanks for sharing this. A small rise can happen — let's review your routine at your visit. In the meantime, keep logging your glucose. If you have symptoms like blurred vision, unusual thirst, or confusion, please contact us or urgent care right away.

Good question about your medication. Don't change your dose on your own — let's discuss it together. If you've missed doses, just resume your normal schedule. Call the office if you have side effects that concern you.

I see your blood pressure readings. Please keep taking your readings at the same time each day. If you get a reading above 180/120 or feel chest pain or shortness of breath, seek emergency care immediately.
Happy to look at your plan. Your trends are helpful — let's set one or two realistic goals for the next few weeks and check progress at your appointment.
Thanks for your message. A member of the care team will follow up. For anything urgent, please call the office or go to urgent care.

assistantIntents.responseTemplate: Your most recent A1c was {value}% on {date}. Your target is {target}. {sourceCitation} {disclaimer}

sampleQuestions: why did my a1c go up?
assistantIntents.responseTemplate: Your A1c went from {prev}% to {value}%. Over the same period your glucose averaged {glucoseAvg} mg/dL. A1c reflects ~3 months. {disclaimer}
Your time in range is {inRangePct}% (target >{target}%) over the last 14 days, averaging {avg} mg/dL. {disclaimer}
You're at {current} lb, {direction} {delta} since {since}. Your goal is {goal} lb. {disclaimer}
Your next appointment option is {slot} with {provider}. Want me to help you book it?
Here are recipes that fit your plan ({filterExplanation}). Opening Recipes…

sampleQuestions: what should i ask my doctor?
assistantIntents.responseTemplate: Here are questions tailored to your data: {questions}. Want me to send these to your care team?
I can answer questions about your labs, medications, activity, weight, appointments, and recipes. Try one of these: {suggestedChips}

assistantDisclaimer: This is a demo assistant, not medical advice. Your care team can help you decide what to do next.

recipes.title: Stuffed Bell Peppers with Brown Rice & Beans

identityDefaults.note: Gate is scripted; no real IdP. Sarah uses 'passkey' by default.

terminologyExamples.disclaimer: ILLUSTRATIVE codes — implementer MUST validate against current LOINC, RxNorm, and SNOMED CT US Edition releases before any real use.

sarah.title: Nephrology consult — visit summary
sarah.bodyText: Assessment: Stage 2 CKD likely secondary to type 2 diabetes. Plan: continue empagliflozin (renal-protective). Titrate metformin to 1000 mg BID. Recheck urine albumin/creatinine in 3 months. Reinforce weight loss and BP control.
sarah.aiRead: Your kidney specialist kept you on empagliflozin because it protects the kidneys, and increased your metformin to 1000 mg twice a day. They want to recheck a urine test in 3 months. Ask your care team if you have questions about the change.

sarah.title: Renal ultrasound report
sarah.bodyText: Findings: Kidneys normal in size with mildly increased echogenicity. No hydronephrosis or stones. Impression: findings compatible with early medical renal disease.
sarah.aiRead: Your kidney ultrasound was essentially normal — no stones or blockage, just mild changes that fit early kidney disease. No action needed from this image alone.

sarah.title: Scanned outside lab — lipid panel
sarah.bodyText: Total cholesterol 188 mg/dL; LDL 102; HDL 48; triglycerides 160.
sarah.aiRead: Your cholesterol from an outside lab was close to goal; triglycerides were slightly high. Diet and activity help here.

sarah.title: Endocrinology note
sarah.bodyText: Type 2 diabetes with obesity. Started semaglutide 0.5 mg weekly. Counseled on diet; goal A1c <7.5%.
sarah.aiRead: Your endocrinologist started semaglutide to help with both blood sugar and weight, with an A1c goal under 7.5%.

robert.title: Primary care — visit summary
robert.bodyText: A1c 7.4%. Continue metformin and empagliflozin. Encourage activity. Recheck A1c in 3 months.
robert.aiRead: At your March visit your A1c was 7.4%. The plan was to keep your medications the same and stay active.

maria.title: Primary care — wellness visit summary
maria.bodyText: A1c 6.1% (pre-diabetes). Weight down 7 lb. Continue MDPP. Recheck A1c in July.
maria.aiRead: At your May visit your A1c was 6.1% — still pre-diabetes but improving with your weight loss. The plan is to keep going with MDPP and recheck in July.

jim.title: Primary care — visit summary
jim.bodyText: A1c 6.6%, in target. Continue metformin 1000 mg BID. Encourage current activity. Recheck in 6 months.
jim.aiRead: Your A1c is 6.6% and in your target range. The plan is to keep your metformin the same and stay active — recheck in about 6 months.

priya.title: Endocrinology — pump visit note
priya.bodyText: Type 1 diabetes on insulin pump + CGM. A1c 7.1%, TIR 68%. Adjust overnight basal to reduce lows. Continue carb counting.
priya.aiRead: Your endocrinologist lowered your overnight insulin a little to reduce nighttime low glucose, and wants you to keep counting carbs. Your time-in-range is close to goal.

hector.title: New-diagnosis visit summary
hector.bodyText: New type 2 diabetes, A1c 9.2%. Start metformin 500 mg with breakfast, titrate. DSMES referral. Follow up 6 weeks.
hector.aiRead: You were just diagnosed with type 2 diabetes and started metformin. The plan is to take it with breakfast, join a diabetes education class, and follow up in 6 weeks. / Le diagnosticaron diabetes tipo 2 y comenzó metformina.

linda.title: Primary care — blood pressure visit
linda.bodyText: Type 2 diabetes + hypertension. A1c 7.8%, home BP avg 146/90. Continue four medications; reinforce low-sodium diet. Consider lisinopril increase if BP stays high.
linda.aiRead: Your blood pressure has been running high, so your doctor may raise one medication if it stays up. Keep taking home readings and watch sodium.

deshawn.title: Primary care — visit summary
deshawn.bodyText: Type 2 diabetes, A1c 7.0%, weight plateau. Continue metformin. Add one activity goal. Recheck 3 months.
deshawn.aiRead: Your A1c is holding at 7.0% and your weight has plateaued. The plan is to keep metformin and add one new activity habit, then recheck in 3 months.

samuel.title: Obesity medicine — visit note
samuel.bodyText: Obesity, class II. On semaglutide 1.0 mg weekly, down 18 lb. Tolerating well. Plan step-up to next dose if side effects remain mild.
samuel.aiRead: You've lost 18 lb on semaglutide and are tolerating it well. Your doctor may step up to the next dose if side effects stay mild.

aisha.title: Primary care — wellness visit
aisha.bodyText: Obesity, class I; prior pre-diabetes resolved (A1c 5.6%). Completed National DPP. Highly active. Continue current plan.
aisha.aiRead: Great news — your A1c moved out of the pre-diabetes range and you completed your prevention program. Keep up your current routine.

carol.title: Physical therapy — plan of care
carol.bodyText: Class III obesity with knee osteoarthritis. Low-impact program: water aerobics 2x/week, seated strength. Adapted step goal 4,000. Reassess in 6 weeks.
carol.aiRead: Your physical therapist set up a joint-friendly plan — water aerobics and seated strength — with a gentler step goal to protect your knees.

miguel.title: Sleep medicine — follow-up note
miguel.bodyText: Obstructive sleep apnea with obesity. CPAP use inconsistent (3.2 hrs/night). Weight plateau. Reinforce CPAP adherence; better sleep supports weight loss.
miguel.aiRead: Using your CPAP more consistently can improve your sleep and help with weight. Your team wants to support getting back on track.

emily.title: Campus health — visit summary
emily.bodyText: Obesity, class I. Vegetarian; peanut and shellfish allergy. Down 5 lb. Balanced plan; monitor iron and B12. Continue.
emily.aiRead: You're down 5 lb on a balanced vegetarian plan. Your dietitian is keeping an eye on iron and B12, which matters on a vegetarian diet.

sarah.title: A pattern worth a question for your doctor
sarah.body: Your urine albumin has risen over your last three labs (28 → 35 → 42 mg/g) while your A1c stayed near 7.5%. Together these can be early signs of diabetes affecting the kidneys — a good thing to ask your care team about.

sarah.title: Your medication plan changed — here's the plain version
sarah.body: Your new nephrology visit summary increases metformin to 1000 mg twice daily and keeps empagliflozin for kidney protection. Take the higher metformin dose with food to reduce stomach upset.

robert.title: Why your A1c moved
robert.body: Your A1c rose from 7.4% to 7.9% as your morning glucose averaged 140 and activity dropped ~22% this week. These move together.

maria.title: Your prevention plan is working
maria.body: Your A1c held at 6.1% while you lost 7 lb and kept up MDPP sessions. Staying on this track lowers your chance of developing type 2 diabetes.

jim.title: A full year in target
jim.body: Your A1c has stayed under 7% for four straight labs while you average 8,000+ steps a day. Whatever you're doing, it's working.

priya.title: A pattern worth a question for your team
priya.body: Your glucose dips low overnight on several days while your time-in-range sits just under goal. Ask your care team whether your overnight basal rate needs a small change.

hector.title: Getting started, step by step
hector.body: Your A1c is high right now, which is common at diagnosis. Taking metformin daily and adding short walks are the two changes that help most in the first months.

linda.title: Blood pressure trending up
linda.body: Your home blood pressure has risen across the last two weeks while your sodium runs above target. These often move together — a good thing to share with your care team.

deshawn.title: Holding steady — and how to nudge it
deshawn.body: Your A1c and weight have been flat for six weeks. One added habit — a 10-minute post-dinner walk — is often enough to restart progress.

samuel.title: Your medication may step up — the plain version
samuel.body: You're down 18 lb and tolerating semaglutide well, so your doctor may increase the dose. Higher doses can increase nausea at first; taking it on a lighter-meal day can help.

aisha.title: Pre-diabetes reversed
aisha.body: Your A1c dropped from 6.0% to 5.6% — out of the pre-diabetes range — alongside your activity streak and weight loss. That's a meaningful reduction in future risk.

carol.title: Gentle movement counts
carol.body: Low-impact activity like water aerobics protects your joints while still helping your weight and blood sugar. Your adapted goals are set with that in mind.

miguel.title: Sleep and weight move together
miguel.body: Your weight has plateaued while your CPAP use has been light. More consistent sleep often makes weight loss easier — a good first focus to restart progress.

emily.title: Steady progress on your plan
emily.body: You're down 5 lb on a vegetarian, allergy-safe plan. Keeping protein and iron up supports energy — your saved recipes already do this well.

sarah.note: QR encodes the mock token only — never real PHI.

symptomCheckins.redFlagResponse: This may need urgent attention. Please contact your care team now, or call 911 / go to urgent care if it's severe. I can't diagnose — but I don't want you to wait on this.

exampleNormal.input: a little tired today and my knees ache
exampleNormal.response: Thanks for logging that — I'll note it for your next visit. Gentle, low-impact activity sometimes helps achy joints.

steps.title: One-tap sign-in
steps.narration: Sarah signs in with a passkey on her phone — IAL2/AAL2, no password. One trusted identity unlocks the whole network.

steps.title: Kill the Clipboard at the specialist
steps.narration: At nephrology check-in she scans a QR code to share her verified FHIR history — no paper forms, no fax.

steps.title: The visit summary returns
steps.narration: After the visit, the summary returns via a Smart Health Link and lands in her records automatically.

steps.title: The AI reads the new record
steps.narration: Her assistant explains the medication titration in plain language and flags the rising kidney marker as a question for her care team.

steps.title: Continuous coaching
steps.narration: Proactive glucose and weight nudges replace waiting for the next 15-minute visit.

steps.title: She owns her data
steps.narration: Sarah reviews exactly who accessed her record, when, and why — treatment vs her own access vs her shares.

note: The two reference personas (maria, robert) and all copy shown on the PROTOTYPE screens MUST be taken verbatim from the embedded prototype sources (#src-prototype-mobile, #src-prototype-desktop). The eleven additionalPersonas (ten varied + featured sarah) and all v1.1/v1.2 feature data above are authoritative for the new screens; render them with the same components, tokens, and chart geometry as the prototypes. Terminology codes are illustrative — validate per terminologyExamples.disclaimer. All v1.2 capabilities are simulated behind the Section 9 swap seams; the runtime makes zero external calls.

socialDriversReference.disclaimer: ILLUSTRATIVE ICD-10-CM Z-codes for health-related social needs (SDOH). The implementer MUST validate against the current ICD-10-CM release (Z55–Z65 family) before any real use. Language access (limited English proficiency) is modeled via preferredLanguage rather than a Z-code.
socialDriversReference.screeningNote: Personas with no need carry an explicit negative screening result (status 'none'), reflecting that HRSN screening was completed.
